preFix.title: Macramé headboard
preFix.subtitle: A large-scale knotted headboard for a double bed, hung from a wooden dowel, approximately 180 x 80 cm
preFix.excerpt: A macramé headboard is a large-scale project mounted on a wooden dowel fixed to the wall above the bed. Cords are knotted in a combination of alternating square knots and diagonal sections, with gathering knots used to create structured column groups. At this scale, managing even tension across a wide working piece is the primary challenge.
preFix.sourceNotes: Macramé headboard construction is consistent across contemporary UK and US macramé instruction. The cord estimate, knot sequence, and mounting method described are standard practice. Observation-led.

content.text: A double-bed macramé headboard requires a significant amount of cord and working time. Cut and weigh cords before beginning: at 3 mm cord for a 180 cm wide headboard with 80 cm of knotted body, expect to use 300 to 400 m of cord total. Cords are mounted to the dowel with
and the body is built from
at intervals to create visual structure across the wide piece.

items.name: 5 mm natural cotton macramé cord for the body knotting
Wooden dowel, 2 cm diameter, 190 cm long
Two D-rings or cord loops for hanging the dowel from the wall
Sturdy rail or clothing rack to hang the dowel during knotting

content.text: Step 1: Cut and mount the cords

Cut 80 lengths of cord each 450 cm long. Fold each length at its midpoint and mount to the dowel with a lark's head knot, positioning the knots at approximately 2.2 cm intervals across 180 cm of the dowel. This gives 80 cords and 160 working ends. Hang the prepared dowel at a comfortable working height (a sturdy clothing rail or two chair backs at the same height works well). Bundle excess cord length with a cording wrap or bundle ties to keep it manageable as you work.

Work the first 50 cm of the headboard in alternating square knots at 4 cm vertical intervals. Use 4-cord groups: tie a square knot in each group, then shift all groupings by 2 cords in the next row. At 50 cm, tie gathering knots across 10 groups of 8 cords each to create structured column breaks across the full width. Each gathering knot is formed by wrapping one cord around 7 bundled cords for 6 tight turns, then threading the working end back up through the wraps.

Below the gathering knots, continue with alternating square knots at wider spacing (6 to 8 cm intervals) for a more open, airy section. Trim all cord ends to a level fringe at 80 cm below the dowel, or cut at varying lengths for a tapered fringe. Unravel the plied cord for 3 to 4 cm above each trimmed end to produce a brushed fringe effect; a stiff brush or pet slicker brush speeds this up.

items.fix: Work from the centre outward in each row rather than left to right. This way you can set the centre tension first and match it at the edges. Check the width across rows with a measuring tape every 10 cm of knotted length.
items.cause: The knotting tension increased toward the centre where the cords are harder to reach
items.problem: The centre of the headboard is tighter than the edges

items.fix: Bundle each cord length into a loose figure-of-eight coil and secure with a slip knot or rubber band, leaving 30 to 40 cm free for knotting. Re-let out cord length as needed.
items.cause: The working cord lengths were not bundled as knotting progressed
items.problem: The cords are tangling badly and difficult to work with
